title: Health | Beehive.govt.nz
content:
## Ground broken on Waikato medical school
Construction is now underway on the Waikato’s new medical school – a major milestone in the Government’s plan to train more GPs and strengthen access to primary care in regional New Zealand.

## Expanding access to everyday medicines
Registered nurse prescribers can now prescribe nearly double the number of medicines, significantly expanding access to timely, local care for New Zealanders, Health Minister Simeon Brown says.

## Urgent care coming to Lower Hutt and Dunedin
More New Zealanders will be able to access urgent care 24/7 before Christmas, with new and expanded services opening in Dunedin and Hutt Valley.

## Pharmac proposal to improve rural access to emergency medicines
Associate Health Minister with responsibility for Pharmac David Seymour, and Associate Health Minister with responsibility for Rural Health Matt Doocey welcome Pharmac’s proposal to fund access to seven medicines for rural emergency care.

## New wraparound counselling service to mark World AIDS Day
Mental Health and Associate Health Minister Matt Doocey today hosted a World AIDS Day event at Parliament, announcing new funding to help more New Zealanders have faster access to mental health support.

## Appointments to the Public Health Advisory Committee
Health Minister Simeon Brown has today announced appointments to the Public Health Advisory Committee, which provides independent advice on public health issues and the promotion of public health.

## Appointments to the Health Workforce Committee
Health Minister Simeon Brown has today announced appointments to the Health Workforce Committee.

## Health New Zealand to welcome 1800 graduate nurses
Health New Zealand’s announcement that around 1800 graduate registered nurses will begin hospital-based roles represents a significant boost for the health system, Health Minister Simeon Brown says.

## Major boost to stem cell transplant services
A significant expansion of stem cell transplant services will help patients access critical, life-saving treatments without delay, Health Minister Simeon Brown says.

## Appointments to the Hauora Māori Advisory Committee
Health Minister Simeon Brown has today announced a series of appointments to the Hauora Māori Advisory Committee.

## Frontline decision-making key to Health New Zealand’s future
Health Minister Simeon Brown has today presented Health New Zealand’s Annual Report to Parliament and issued a new Letter of Expectations to the Chair of the Health New Zealand Board.

## Digitising healthcare to put patients first
Repeating your medical history at every appointment will soon be a thing of the past, with Kiwis set to get faster access to care as part of a 10-year plan to bring New Zealand's health system into the digital age.

## New CT scanner boosting output at Kenepuru Hospital
The new Photon Counting Computed Tomography (PCCT) scanner at Kenepuru Hospital, which opened last month, is already delivering significant benefits for patients in Porirua and the surrounding communities.

## New Dunedin Hospital hits first milestone
The New Dunedin Hospital project has reached a significant construction milestone, with pile capping for the new inpatient building now successfully completed, Health Minister Simeon Brown says.

## New safeguards for puberty blocker prescribing
Cabinet has agreed to introduce new safeguards on the prescribing of gonadotropin-releasing hormone analogues, while ensuring patients with medical needs can continue to access appropriate care.

## Smoking rate reduces to 6.8 per cent
Associate Health Minister Casey Costello has welcomed the latest New Zealand Health Survey (NZHS) results showing smoking rates reducing to 6.8 per cent.

## State-of-the-art $16 million operating theatre for Wellington
Health New Zealand has approved $16.25 million in funding for the establishment of a new advanced hybrid operating theatre, which will allow patients at Wellington Regional Hospital to access faster, safer, and more advanced surgical care.

## Nurse practitioners to deliver faster access to medicines
Nurse practitioners can now prescribe a wider range of medicines, helping New Zealanders get faster access to the medicines they need and improving continuity of care, Health Minister Simeon Brown says.

## New Wellington stroke pathway speeds up recovery
A new stroke pathway at Wellington Regional Hospital is delivering faster rehabilitation and better outcomes for stroke patients, Health Minister Simeon Brown says.

## Sod turned on Whangārei Hospital car park extension
A key milestone has been reached in the redevelopment of Whangārei Hospital, with the sod turning for a major on-grade car parking extension to enable construction of the new acute services building.

## Easier and cheaper prescriptions for Kiwis
From 1 February 2026, New Zealanders will be able to receive 12-month prescriptions for their medicines, delivering savings and greater convenience for people on long-term treatments.

## First cancer medicine available in private hospitals
Associate Health Minister with responsibility for Pharmac David Seymour, and Health Minister Simeon Brown welcome Pharmac’s decision to fund access to five treatments, including for breast and lung cancer, from 1 December 2025.

## Medical conference red tape cut, and NZ is open for business
The passing on the Medicines Amendment Bill has ended New Zealand’s prohibition on advertising medicines at medical conferences and trade shows, and New Zealand is open for business.

## Medicines Amendment Bill passes third reading
Associate Health Minister David Seymour is welcoming the passing of the Medicines Amendment Bill at third reading.
